patches.find: Simple geometry: two shadow rays through cloud gaps separated by 5 km at 10 km altitude subtend a ~0.05° angular difference at ground level — a difference that only widens as the observer turns away from the sun. Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km. Simple geometry: two shadow rays through cloud gaps separated by 5 km at 10 km altitude subtend a ~0.05° angular difference at ground level — a difference that only widens as the observer turns away from the sun. Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km.
patches.replace: Simple geometry: two shadow rays through cloud gaps separated by 5 km at 10 km altitude subtend a ~0.05° angular difference at ground level — a difference that only widens as the observer turns away from the sun. That 0.05° is ~9% of the Sun's angular diameter — small enough that a local 5,733 km sun would look near-parallel for short baselines, but converging rays at the anti-solar point require the rays to remain straight over the full sky hemisphere, which is geometrically incompatible with any finite source. Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km.
patches.rationale: Removes verbatim duplicate paragraph (copy-paste artifact). Also adds clarifying sentence connecting 0.05° calculation to anti-solar argument (addresses ISS-1788 moderate hole simultaneously). Combined into one patch to avoid stale find strings after first patch applies.

patches.find: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test on structural grounds. The numerical value is textbook physics (Part 3.1b, "trivial-transcription test"): it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources, so there is no meaningful sense in which the dome monitor script "predicted" it before reading the data. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call, making even that weaker test unverifiable. No legitimate-novelty carve-out applies: the value is not obscure and the derivation (where one is offered at all) is algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. See Part 3.1b.
patches.replace: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test on structural grounds. monitor.py hardcodes both expected and observed values to the same dome-internal parameter (5,733 km), so the comparison is a self-equality with no independent measurement. Anticrepuscular convergence at the anti-solar point — the actual empirical content of the WIN — is never tested by the script; it cannot be, because the dome's own model documentation (05-model.txt) concedes the 5,733 km figure is an optical illusion rather than a physical altitude. There is no operational sense in which this is a prospective prediction of a real measurement. See Part 3.1b for the broader pattern of hardcoded-on-both-sides WINs.
patches.rationale: Replaces misapplied trivial-transcription test with correct structural post-hoc critique. 5,733 km is a dome-internal parameter, not textbook physics. The correct critique is hardcoded-both-sides self-equality.
Mathematical perspective (vanishing-point projection) is a Renaissance development ~1413-1435, not antiquity. Avoids overclaim while preserving the gist.
